name: nexus/compliance
description: Regulatory-compliance marker detection across five subcategories: gdpr (data-subject-rights, right-to-erasure, lawful-basis, cross-border transfer), hipaa (PHI handling phrases, BAA references, minimum-necessary / covered-entity language), pci (cardholder-data-environment markers, prohibited sensitive-auth storage, PAN-in-the-clear context, DSS scope references), regulated_advice (medical / legal / financial / tax / insurance advice-seeking that needs a licensed professional), and consent (consent / opt-in / opt-out / withdrawal / do-not-call / do-not-sell language). This pack flags COMPLIANCE-RELEVANT natural-language markers and framework references — NOT the raw regulated values themselves: card / SSN / MRN structure lives in nexus/pii, API keys and credentials in nexus/secret-leak, bulk-exfil and classification banners in nexus/data-loss, and harmful content in nexus/content-safety. Severity is deliberately advisory (mostly soft/warn) so these drive flag/route, not hard-block; short ambiguous acronyms (DSAR/PHI/BAA) are kept only where the token is specific enough or paired with a spelled form to bound prose false-positives. Patterns are Vectorscan-compatible: Go RE2 syntax, no backreferences, no lookaround, bounded quantifiers, word-boundary anchored (no bare ^/$), explicit ASCII classes over `.`.

name: nexus/tool-call-safety
description: Tool-call safety patterns for agent / function-calling traffic. Day-1 rules cover shell destructive ops, filesystem sensitive paths, SSRF (cloud metadata + RFC1918 + k8s service names), SQL injection heuristics, code-evaluation primitives, dangerous deserialization, destructive DB DDL, Kubernetes destructive verbs, and credential env-var exfiltration. All rules emit `detector:tool-call-safety` plus a finer `risk:` label so downstream policies can filter by risk class.

name: nexus/data-loss
description: Data-loss-prevention patterns for confidential-document leakage and bulk-data exfiltration through the gateway. Five subcategories: classification markers (CONFIDENTIAL / TRADE SECRET / ATTORNEY-CLIENT / TLP / ITAR-NOFORN), encoded payloads (large base64, data: URIs, long hex, archive magic), bulk dumps (SQL dump headers, multi-tuple INSERT, PII CSV headers, internal DB connection strings), source-code leakage (unified diffs, credentialed repo URLs, internal registries), and internal references (corp hostnames, SharePoint/Confluence URLs, ticket keys in a confidential context). Scope excludes API keys (nexus/secret-leak), personal data (nexus/pii), and harmful content. Every rule emits `detector:data-loss` plus a finer marker:/vector: label and a `severity:<tier>` label so downstream policies can filter by exfil class.

name: nexus/jailbreak
description: Known jailbreak families — DAN variants, developer-mode, grandma/research pretexts. Shares pattern substrings with nexus/prompt-injection but is shipped as a distinct pack so admins can enable/disable independently.

name: nexus/supply-chain
description: Software-supply-chain risk detection for code and shell snippets flowing through the gateway. Five subcategories: install-script abuse (postinstall / preinstall hooks that pipe a remote download to a shell, base64-decode-and-run, or eval inline at install time), dependency confusion (package-manager registry / index-url repointed at an attacker host or raw IP), malicious packages (documented npm / PyPI typosquats, installs from unvetted git / URL / archive sources, root-privileged install scripts), cryptominers (miner binaries, the stratum pool protocol, Monero wallet addresses, known mining-pool hosts), and build-time exfiltration (environment dumps or credential files shipped to a remote host inside an install / CI step). Distinct from nexus/tool-call-safety (runtime tool-call abuse) and nexus/insecure-code (code-quality weaknesses): this pack targets the dependency / build supply chain. Grounded in real incidents (crossenv / jeIlyfish / python3-dateutil typosquats, npm postinstall malware, dependency-confusion research, OWASP CI/CD Top 10). Every rule emits `detector:supply-chain` plus a finer `risk:` label and a `severity:<tier>` label so downstream policies can filter by supply-chain class. Severities are hard for install-time pipe-to-shell and build exfiltration; soft for name / source heuristics.

name: nexus/content-safety
description: Content-safety patterns across 5 restricted categories: violence, hate_speech, self_harm, sexual, illegal. Each rule emits `detector:content-safety`, `category:<name>`, and `severity:restricted` labels. Patterns are intentionally contextual (verb + target / how-to framing) to keep idiomatic business text — "kill the process", "attack the problem", "human trafficking survivor", "the book covers the Holocaust" — out of the match set. Severity defaults to `soft`; the P-E content-safety hook escalates via AI Guard when configured.

name: nexus/cloud-misconfig
description: Insecure cloud / infrastructure-as-code configuration detection for text and code flowing through the gateway — model-generated or user-pasted Terraform, CloudFormation, Kubernetes manifests, Docker run/compose, and cloud CLI/JSON. Six subcategories: public_access (world-readable/writable buckets, AllUsers/AllAuthenticatedUsers grants, CloudFormation public canned ACLs, 0.0.0.0/0 ingress including sensitive admin/database ports, GCP allUsers bindings, disabled S3 public access blocks, publicly-accessible databases), iam (wildcard Action/Resource, Principal "*" trust policies, administrator-tier policy attach, service-wide wildcards, Allow+NotAction, "*:*" full wildcard), encryption (encryption-at-rest disabled, anonymous blob access, public Azure containers, weak TLS, disabled TLS verification), iac_secret (hardcoded cloud access keys, passwords, private keys, Terraform provider creds, GCP service-account key JSON, and Azure client secrets embedded in IaC), k8s (privileged containers, host namespaces, hostPath, run-as-root, privilege escalation, dangerous Linux capabilities, automounted service-account tokens), and container (Docker --privileged, --cap-add of dangerous capabilities, host networking). Grounded in CIS Benchmarks and the tfsec/checkov/kube-score misconfig catalogs. Distinct from nexus/insecure-code (application-code weaknesses) and nexus/secret-leak (raw credential strings): this pack flags misconfigured cloud INFRASTRUCTURE. Every rule emits `detector:cloud-misconfig` plus a `cloud:<platform>` label and a `severity:<tier>` data-classification label so downstream policies can filter by platform and exposure class.

name: nexus/pii
description: Regulated personal-data (PII / PHI) detection across 4 subcategories: financial, government_id, contact, health. This pack is scoped to PERSONAL / regulated data only — API keys and credentials live in nexus/secret-leak, harmful content in nexus/content-safety. Patterns are intentionally contextual: loosely-structured values (account / MRN / passport / DL / national-id) require a nearby label word AND at least one digit so idiomatic prose ("the medical record system", "driver license bureau", "tax id requirement") stays out of the match set. Card / SSN rules prefer either a label-word context OR strict separated structure; Luhn / check-digit validation is the detector hook's job, not the regex. Severity defaults to soft/warn — PII detection drives redact/flag, not hard-block. Patterns are Vectorscan-compatible: Go RE2 syntax, no backreferences, no lookaround, bounded quantifiers, word-boundary anchored (no bare ^/$).

name: nexus/insecure-code
description: Insecure-code-construct detection for LLM-generated and user-supplied code flowing through the gateway. Day-1 rules are CWE-aligned weak-output checks: weak crypto (MD5/SHA1/DES/RC4/AES-ECB/hardcoded IV), hardcoded secrets in code, disabled TLS verification, injection-prone string construction (SQL/OS-command), insecure randomness in a security context, and unsafe deserialization. Distinct from nexus/tool-call-safety: that pack flags runtime tool-call ABUSE (an action the agent is about to take); this pack flags code-quality / security WEAKNESSES that the code itself ships. All rules emit `detector:insecure-code` plus a `cwe:` label so downstream policies can filter by weakness class. Severities are advisory (soft/warn) except for unambiguous TLS-verification disablement (hard).

name: nexus/malware-threat
description: Defensive detection signatures for malware / offensive-tooling indicators in text flowing through the gateway — a model being asked to emit malware, or malicious code / IOCs pasted into a prompt. These are DETECTORS (in the spirit of Suricata / Sigma / YARA signatures), not payloads: each rule flags a well-known malicious construct so the pipeline can block, redact, or route to review. Six subcategories: reverse_shell, obfuscation, c2, dropper, ransomware, exploit. Distinct from nexus/tool-call-safety (runtime tool-call abuse) and nexus/insecure-code (shipped code weaknesses) — this pack is offensive-artifact detection. Patterns are Vectorscan-compatible (Go RE2, no backref/lookaround, bounded quantifiers, no bare ^/$). Severity is hard for unambiguous offensive constructs, soft/warn for heuristics with benign collisions.

name: nexus/secret-leak
description: API key and credential patterns for common providers. Day-1 list covers AWS / GCP / Azure / OpenAI / Anthropic / GitHub / Stripe / Slack / SSH / JWT + an optional entropy-gated generic bearer pattern. All rules emit severity:restricted so the pipeline-level severity picker escalates.

name: nexus/prompt-injection
description: Nexus-curated prompt-injection patterns. Covers instruction override, role-play escapes, system-prompt leakage, delimiter confusion, and encoded-payload variants. Day-1 scope is conservative; tune FP after production telemetry.
